questions.question: Explain the divide-and-conquer approach in Quick Sort with the three main steps.
keyPoints: Divide: Select a pivot element and partition the array around it
Conquer: Recursively sort the two subarrays (elements less than and greater than pivot)
Combine: No explicit combination step needed as elements are sorted in-place during partitioning

questions.question: Derive the average-case time complexity of Quick Sort using recurrence relation.
keyPoints: Average case: T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + Θ(n) where k is number of elements on one side
Assuming all pivot positions equally likely, expected k = (n-1)/2
Solving T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/2) + Θ(n) yields T(n) = Θ(n log n)
Each level does O(n) work, and there are O(log n) levels in balanced partition

questions.question: Compare and contrast Quick Sort and Merge Sort based on: (i) time complexity, (ii) space complexity, (iii) stability, (iv) in-place property.
keyPoints: Time: Both O(n log n) average; Quick Sort O(n²) worst, Merge Sort always O(n log n)
Space: Quick Sort O(log n) for recursion, Merge Sort O(n) auxiliary for merge
Stability: Quick Sort is NOT stable, Merge Sort IS stable
In-place: Quick Sort is in-place, Merge Sort requires O(n) extra space

questions.question: Explain Lomuto partition scheme with an example array [7, 2, 1, 6, 8, 5, 3, 4] using last element as pivot.
keyPoints: Pivot = 4 (last element)
Initialize i = -1 (index of smallest element boundary)
Iterate: for each element < 4, increment i and swap
After iteration, swap pivot with element at i+1
Result: [2, 1, 3, 4, 8, 5, 7, 6] with 4 at its correct sorted position

questions.question: What is randomized Quick Sort? How does it help avoid worst-case behavior?
keyPoints: Randomized Quick Sort randomly selects the pivot (typically by swapping with random element before partitioning)
By randomizing, input distribution doesn't affect performance - all input permutations equally likely
Expected time remains O(n log n) regardless of input order
Prevents adversarial inputs that cause O(n²) behavior in deterministic Quick Sort

questions.question: Why is Quick Sort preferred over Merge Sort for array sorting in practice?
keyPoints: Quick Sort is in-place requiring only O(log n) auxiliary space vs O(n) for Merge Sort
Better cache performance due to in-place sorting and locality of reference
Lower constant factors in practice despite same asymptotic complexity
Merge Sort requires extra memory allocation overhead

questions.question: Trace through Quick Sort step-by-step on the array [8, 3, 6, 4, 9, 2, 1, 5] showing all recursive calls and partitions when using first element as pivot.
keyPoints: First call: pivot=8, partition: [3,6,4,2,1,5,8,9], pivot at position 6
Left subarray [3,6,4,2,1,5]: pivot=3, partition: [1,2,3,6,4,5], pivot at position 2
Continue with right of 3: [6,4,5]: pivot=6, partition: [4,5,6]
Left of 3: [1,2]: partition: [1,2,3]
Final sorted array: [1,2,3,4,5,6,8,9]
Show all recursive call stack and partition operations

questions.question: Derive the worst-case time complexity of Quick Sort and explain when this worst case occurs. Also explain two techniques to avoid the worst case.
keyPoints: Worst case T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + Θ(n) = T(n-1) + Θ(n), solving gives Θ(n²)
Occurs when pivot is always smallest or largest element (highly unbalanced partition)
Examples: sorted array with first/last element pivot, array in reverse order
Technique 1: Randomized Quick Sort - random pivot selection gives expected O(n log n)
Technique 2: Median-of-three pivot - choose median of first, middle, last elements
Technique 3: Introsort - switch to Heap Sort when recursion depth exceeds threshold
Technique 4: Tail call optimization - limit recursion depth to O(log n)
